sublevels.title: FILE I/O
sublevels.subtitle: Reading and writing files with stdio.h

teaching.title: C File I/O
teaching.analogy: C file I/O is like using a cassette tape — you press play (fopen), interact with it sequentially (read/write), and press stop (fclose). Miss fclose and you risk losing data or leaving the tape jammed. No convenience — just raw control.
teaching.content: FILE* pointer from <stdio.h>. fopen(name, mode) opens. fclose(fp) closes. Modes: 'r' read, 'w' write (truncates), 'a' append, 'rb'/'wb' binary. fprintf, fscanf, fgets, fputs for reading/writing. Always check fopen != NULL.

examples.title: Writing to a File
Reading from a File

teaching.funFact: stdin, stdout, stderr are FILE* pointers too! printf is fprintf(stdout,...), scanf is fscanf(stdin,...). C treats everything as a file stream — even keyboard input and screen output follow the same FILE* model! 🖥️

teaching.title: fgets, fscanf & Binary I/O
teaching.analogy: fgets is safer than gets() — it limits how many characters it reads, preventing buffer overflow. It's the difference between a bouncer who checks IDs (fgets) and one who lets everyone in (gets — never use!).
teaching.content: fgets(buffer, size, fp): reads until newline or size-1 chars. fscanf: formatted read (like scanf). fread/fwrite: binary I/O for raw data structures. fprintf with FILE* for formatted write. feof() checks end of file.

examples.title: fscanf and fwrite

teaching.funFact: The infamous gets() function (never use it!) was so dangerous that C11 actually REMOVED it from the standard library. It's the only function ever removed from the C standard! fgets() is its safe replacement. ⚠️

options: File can't be opened (doesn't exist for 'r', permission denied, etc.)
Always returns NULL for text files
mcq.explanation: Error: fopen returns NULL on failure. ALWAYS check: if(!fp){perror('error');return 1;}. Proceeding with NULL fp causes undefined behavior.

mcq.q: fopen mode 'w' does what if file already exists?
mcq.explanation: Truncate: Mode 'w' opens for writing and TRUNCATES the file if it exists. Use 'a' for append. Use 'r+' for read+write without truncation.

mcq.q: What function reads a line safely with buffer limit?
mcq.explanation: fgets(): fgets(buf, size, fp) reads at most size-1 characters. gets() has NO size limit — it's been removed from C11 due to buffer overflow risk!

mcq.q: Why must you call fclose() after writing?
options: Flushes write buffer to disk and releases file handle
mcq.explanation: Flush + Release: C buffers writes. fclose() flushes the buffer to disk (data actually saved) and releases the OS file handle. Without fclose, data may be lost!

options: Pointer to a FILE structure (file descriptor abstraction)
mcq.explanation: FILE Struct: FILE is a structure defined in stdio.h holding file state. FILE* is a pointer to it. stdin, stdout, stderr are pre-defined FILE* pointers.

coding.title: fopen write mode
coding.hint: 'w' mode opens for writing, creates or truncates.

coding.title: NULL check
coding.objective: After fopen, check if it failed:
coding.hint: fopen returns NULL on failure. Check == NULL.

coding.title: fgets buffer read
coding.objective: Read line from fp into char line[100]:

options: File opened in 'r' (read) mode. Writing to it is undefined behavior. Use 'w' or 'a' mode to write.
debugging.answer: File opened in 'r' (read) mode. Writing to it is undefined behavior. Use 'w' or 'a' mode to write.
debugging.hint: 'r' = read only. 'w' = write. 'a' = append. Writing to a read-only file is undefined behavior.

prediction.explanation: 'w' creates/truncates. Both fprintf calls write. fclose flushes. File has both lines.

teaching.title: C Error Handling
teaching.analogy: C has no exceptions — errors are just return values and a global 'errno' flag. Like a surgeon who signals 'failed' by returning -1 and leaving a note (errno) next to the patient. You MUST check the return value — C won't force you.
teaching.content: C error handling: return codes (0 = success, non-zero = error, -1 = Unix convention). errno (from <errno.h>): set by system calls on error. perror() prints human-readable error. strerror(errno) gets error string. assert() for debug checks.

examples.title: Return Codes & errno
examples.explanation: Check return values! C won't throw for you.

examples.title: assert for Debug Checks
examples.explanation: assert crashes with message if condition false.

teaching.funFact: C's lack of exceptions is intentional — exceptions have hidden performance costs. C programmers check EVERY return value (or they should!). Linux system calls return -1 on error and set errno. No exceptions ever! ⚡

options: Return codes (often -1 or NULL) and errno global
mcq.explanation: Return Codes: C has no exceptions. Errors are communicated via return values (-1, NULL, non-zero). Check every function return! errno provides more detail.

options: Prints a human-readable error message combining your prefix and errno
mcq.explanation: perror: Prints your prefix + ': ' + system error string from errno. 'fopen: No such file or directory' — combines your context with the OS error message.

mcq.q: assert(x > 0) when x = -5 in debug build:
options: Terminates program with assertion failure message and file/line info
mcq.explanation: Abort: assert(condition) calls abort() if condition is false. Prints: 'Assertion failed: x>0, file main.c, line 5'. Used for catching impossible states in debug.
0 = Success: C convention (from Unix): 0 = success, non-zero = error. main() returns 0 on success. system(), fclose(), fflush() all return 0 on success.

coding.title: Return code success
coding.objective: C convention: return ___ on success from a function.

coding.title: perror usage
coding.objective: After failed fopen, print error with context 'File error':
coding.hint: perror(prefix) prints prefix + system error message from errno.

options: fopen could return NULL — passing NULL to fgets is undefined behavior. Always check: if(!fp){return 1;} before using fp.
debugging.answer: fopen could return NULL — passing NULL to fgets is undefined behavior. Always check: if(!fp){return 1;} before using fp.
debugging.hint: Never use fp without checking if fopen succeeded (returned non-NULL).

teaching.title: Dynamic Memory in C
teaching.analogy: malloc is like renting storage units — you request the size you need, get a key (pointer), and MUST return it when done (free). Forget to return the key and the storage stays locked forever (memory leak). Return the wrong key and chaos ensues (double-free, use-after-free).
teaching.content: malloc(size): allocates size bytes on heap, returns void*. calloc(n,size): allocates n*size bytes, zeroed. realloc(ptr,size): resize allocation. free(ptr): MUST be called or memory leaks. Check for NULL on allocation failure.

examples.title: malloc and free
calloc & realloc

teaching.funFact: Memory leaks in long-running programs (servers, kernels) accumulate until the OS kills the process or system crashes. NASA missions use strict 'no dynamic allocation' rules in flight software — all memory decided at compile time! 🚀

options: Pointer to 100 bytes of heap memory (uninitialized)
mcq.explanation: Heap Pointer: malloc returns a void* pointing to 100 bytes of HEAP memory. Content is UNINITIALIZED (garbage). Cast to needed type: int* arr = (int*)malloc(n*sizeof(int));

mcq.q: What happens if you forget to call free()?
options: Memory leak — heap memory never returned to OS until program ends
mcq.explanation: Memory Leak: The allocated block remains reserved for the process lifetime. In long-running programs, leaks grow until system runs out of memory.

options: calloc zeroes the memory; malloc leaves it uninitialized
mcq.explanation: Zeroed: calloc initializes all bytes to 0. malloc leaves memory uninitialized (garbage values). calloc is safer but slightly slower due to zeroing.

mcq.q: After free(ptr), what should you do to prevent use-after-free?
options: Set ptr = NULL to prevent accidental reuse
mcq.explanation: Null After Free: Set ptr = NULL after free(ptr). Accidentally using a freed pointer is undefined behavior. A NULL ptr dereference crashes immediately — a much cleaner failure!

coding.title: malloc for 10 ints
coding.objective: Allocate array of 10 ints on heap:
coding.hint: malloc(bytes) allocates that many bytes on the heap.

coding.title: Always free
coding.objective: After using arr (malloced), what must you call?
coding.hint: free() returns heap memory. Not calling it = memory leak.

options: Calling free twice on same pointer is undefined behavior — heap corruption. Set p=NULL after first free.
debugging.answer: Calling free twice on same pointer is undefined behavior — heap corruption. Set p=NULL after first free.
debugging.hint: After free(p), set p=NULL. free(NULL) is safe and does nothing — prevents accidental double-free.

prediction.explanation: calloc zeroes all. p[0]=1, p[1]=2 set. p[2] stays 0 (calloc zeroed). Output: 1 2 0.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE C SYSTEM
teaching.analogy: The C System manages files, errors, and memory simultaneously — the three pillars of C systems programming. Miss any one and you leak resources, corrupt memory, or lose data. Prove you can handle all three!
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 6 C: fopen modes/NULL check, fclose flush, fprintf/fgets, return codes (0=success), errno/perror, assert, malloc/free/NULL check, calloc zeros, realloc, double-free danger.

examples.title: Full C Systems Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ FILE *fp = fopen(name,mode) — check NULL!
✅ 'r'=read, 'w'=write+truncate, 'a'=append
✅ fclose() flushes buffer to disk
✅ fprintf(fp,...) / fgets(buf,n,fp)
✅ Return 0 = success, -1 = error
✅ perror() prints errno message
✅ assert(cond) crashes in debug if false
✅ malloc(n*sizeof(T)) — check NULL!
✅ calloc zeroes, malloc doesn't
✅ free(ptr); ptr=NULL; — prevent double-free

teaching.funFact: Valgrind is a tool that detects memory leaks and invalid memory accesses in C programs. Running valgrind on large codebases can reveal thousands of leaks in 'working' programs. Essential for C development! 🔍

mcq.explanation: Creates: 'w' mode creates the file if it doesn't exist, truncates if it does. Only 'r' mode requires the file to exist.

options: Flushes write buffers to disk and releases OS file descriptor
mcq.explanation: Critical: fclose flushes C's write buffer — bytes might be buffered in C's stdio, not yet written to disk. Without fclose, last writes may be LOST.

options: Allocation FAILED — malloc returned NULL. Handle the error!
mcq.explanation: Allocation Failed: malloc returns NULL when system can't allocate memory. ALWAYS check and handle this case before using the pointer!

mcq.q: After free(ptr), ptr still holds the old address. Using *ptr:
mcq.explanation: Undefined Behavior: The memory was returned to the heap — it may be reallocated. Dereferencing a freed pointer is undefined behavior and a common security vulnerability.

mcq.q: calloc(5, sizeof(double)) allocates how many bytes? (double=8)
mcq.explanation: 40 bytes: calloc(n, size) = n × size = 5 × 8 = 40 bytes, all zeroed.

coding.title: Boss: Complete file write
coding.objective: Open 'out.txt' for writing, check failure, write 'Hello':

coding.title: Boss: malloc + free
coding.objective: Allocate 20 ints. Last step after using arr?
coding.hint: Always free heap-allocated memory when done.

options: fgets limit (1000) exceeds buf size (5) — buffer overflow! Use: fgets(buf, sizeof(buf), fp)
debugging.answer: fgets limit (1000) exceeds buf size (5) — buffer overflow! Use: fgets(buf, sizeof(buf), fp)
debugging.hint: fgets second arg must be <= buffer size. Always use sizeof(buf) to be safe.

prediction.explanation: calloc: *n starts as 0. *n=10 sets to 10. *n+=5: 10+5=15. free and null-set are correct.
